segments.text: Right. Uh,
Welcome to Table.
Uh, we're in your heads.
Sure, Koto. My name's Andrew Eton.
I'm the Deputy Chair of the New Zealand Council
for Civil Liberties and an adjunct senior research fellow at
Victoria, university of Wellington and School of Government.
Uh, N-Z-T-C-L is a non-partisan voluntary organization
established in 1952, which advocates for human rights
and the maintenance and civil liberties.
Um, this is our second time we've
submitted standing Orders review.
The first time was three years ago,
and, uh, unfortunately, many
of the issues we raised three years ago is still relevant
now in our consideration.
Um, but we sent a fairly substantial written submission
about the issues that concern us, uh, to the committee,
and we assume that it's been read.
Um, we, uh,
are particularly concerned about the independence
of select committees from the executive
and for the legislature command public trust
and confidence, which is an issue
for democracies around the world.
It has to be seen to be acting independently.
Uh, members of this committee will be aware
of the exchanges over the last two days regarding the Chair
of the Environment Committee curtailing the time
for submissions on the fast
track approval of Amendment Bill.
And indeed, I watched the, uh,
the exchange at question time today.
Um, the, the council's concerned that the chair
of the committee allowed the desire of the government
to get a bill on the statute book by the end of the year
to override normal timeframes for public input on the bill.
Uh, it looked to us like it was a,
a lighting, if I can put it politely,
of the requirement in standing orders for there
to be a debate at first reading on report
back shorter than four months.
And, uh, in turn, that makes it look like the independence
of the select committee processes being compromised,
or that there was a desire by the government
to avoid debate on a shorter time report back.
This suggests to us that standing orders need to be amended
to explicitly protect against the chair
or committee significantly curtailing IE not reporting back
by a day or two early the time for evidence to be submitted
and considered by the select committee.
For example, standing order 3 0 3 states
that committees must report after six months,
unless a shorter period is instructed by the house,
and there's no explicit power for a committee
to shorten a report back.
We think this is a good place for a clarifying amendment
to make it clear that shortening consideration
below the level agreed by the house
requires a motion in the house.
Besides this recent incident,
our submission sets out our longstanding concerns
regarding analysis of submissions to select committees.
Um, in particular, uh,
and we state point this out in paragraph 52
of our written submission, David McGee,
in the fourth edition of parliamentary practice in
New Zealand said that although advisors,
although government officials are advisors to the committee,
their primary duty of public servants is
to their respective ministers.
And it is always for ministers to decide whether
to make available officials available
as advisors and which officials.
So, although they're there to support the committee,
their primary duty is to support the government
and the government's mission is to get the bill through.
Um, we raised this concern in our,
uh, submission to the 2023 standing oil was reviewed based
on our experience since then,
however, at least two select committees have public
expressed their unhappiness at government officials acting
contrary to committee's wishes, IE the
concerns that we raised.
Um, for example, in June, 2023, newsroom reported
that the Department of Internal Affairs had acted contrary
to the wishes of the Finance and Expenditure Committee.
When considering the legislation on three Waters,
and the committee's report stated, we wish to put on record
that we did experience some problems during our gruesome
of the bill and the preparation of this report to the House.
We are concerned that there were instances where it appeared
that departmental officials perhaps inadvertently
directed Parliamentary council to make changes
to the revision track version of the bill
that were either not clearly authorized by us
or ran contrary to recommendations.
And two months later, the Herald reported that MBI had
to apologize to the Foreign Affairs Defense
and Trade Select Committee
after what our chair here described as devious behavior.
The select committee was split,
and so did not call for a departmental report in its
consideration of the bill, but MB officials nevertheless
prepared a report which the immigration minister said he
would use to inform any changes.
So a Green Party member of
that select committee commented on the MB Chief Executive's
explanation saying this was not a process error.
I think that with respect, you need
to stop characterizing in that way.
They knew that the committee did not want to report.
This is a real problem in terms of how the public perceive
and can have confidence in the legislature acting
independently of the executive
and standing up for New Zealanders, having their views
properly scrutinized and reported when making
submissions to a select committee.
Now previously, resourcing for Parliament
to employ its own select committee advisors has been
hampered by government control of Parliament's budget.
But passage of the Parliament Bill changes this so
that we think the Standing Orders Review Committee should
make recommendations to that the committee
that will be considering Parliament budget setting, uh,
provide the clerk with funding
to employ significantly more officials
to guarantee Parliamentary officials provide this analysis
to MPS and not government officials.
And we say in our written submission that if the,
if this committee doesn't want to go that far,
then it should be commissioning the parliamentary library
to do comparative research on legislatures around the world,
particularly Westminster,
but I think also Ireland, the United States to say
what resourcing is given to the,
the parliamentary officials in those literature so
that the independence of select committee scrutiny
of bills is protected.
We also talk about the issues in our submission with, uh,
the rise of, uh, the use of technology to,
to both make submissions on bills
and analyze submissions on bills.
Um, I won't go into this in great detail
because we've, we've set it out on page 17
and following pages of our submission.
But we, uh, recommend that, uh,
parliament should be exploring alternative uses
of technology to build public trust in democratic processes
and to facilitate public input in ways that don't lead
to the overwhelming committee with tens
of thousands of submissions.
Uh, we say you should commission the parliamentary library
to provide it with a report on processes
and technologies used in other countries
to facilitate meaningful public participation in development
and scrutiny of legislation.
Um, in terms of urgency, you will be unsurprised to hear
that we're, uh, quite concerned about the use of it, uh,
and the increasing use of it.
But we should be clear also that, uh,
other governments have, in our view, misused urgency.
Uh, in our exper We, we experienced this
during the Government Blast Government's response to COVID.
That was the circumstance in which some use
of urgency was clearly understandable,
but there were circumstances in which it was used
for measures that had major civil liberties implications,
such as vaccine passes, where there was no publication
of policy papers or a description of how
that system would work before parliamentary debate.
And when we made OIA requests, knowing
that this was coming up
and was going to put through under urgency,
government did not respond to provide that information
to enable us to analyze the material,
let alone inform members
of the house on the potential civil liberties implications
of, of the system that was being introduced.
Um, we also recommend
that if a bill is passed under urgency,
then standing orders should require the immediate posten
enactment scrutiny of that act by a select committee.
And we note that Professor Dean Knight of Victoria,
university of Wellington, makes a similar recommendation,
uh, in his submission.
Um, fundamentally, we think
that there's a real problem in New Zealand's political
system with select committees being the focus for the public
to have their input into policy development laws.
We have a very poor culture of exposure drafts

if there's been no public consultation on exposure draft the
